titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the David Shapiro Papers
titlestmt.author: by Nora Soto

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday September 7, 2023 at 03:11

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 42 (student records): Restricted until 2077. For further information consult the appropriate curator.
Box 50 (computer media): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies of electronic files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 51 (audiovisual materials): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from James S. Jaffe on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2019.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Correspondence, 1957-2017. II. Writings, 1957-2017. III. Chronological Files, circa 1983-2011. IV. Personal and Professional Papers, circa 1950s-2016. V. Computer and Audiovisual Media, 1974-1992.

bioghist.head: David Shapiro (1947-)
bioghist.p: David Shapiro is an American poet, writer, art critic and historian, literary critic, editor, and educator associated with the second generation of the New York School of poetry. He has published over twenty-five books of poetry, art criticism, literary criticism, and works of translation, including An Anthology of New York Poets (1970), co-edited with Ron Padgett. He was born on January 2, 1947 in Newark, New Jersey and was raised there. He spent his summers in Deal, New Jersey. His grandfather was Jewish cantor and composer Berele Chagy. Shapiro studied the violin from an early age, performing professionally with orchestras as a teenager. He attended Columbia University, where he was a student of Kenneth Koch and Meyer Schapiro, and participated in the 1968 student protests. After graduating with a B.A. in English in 1968, Shapiro earned an M.A. in English literature and Greek tragedy from Cambridge University in 1970, and a Ph.D. in English and Comparative Literature from Columbia in 1973.

Shapiro began writing poetry when he was nine years old and published his first poetry collection, January: A Book of Poems (1965), at age eighteen. His other poetry volumes include The Page-Turner (1973), After a Lost Original (1994), and New and Selected Poems, 1965–2006 (2007). His poems have been anthologized in several editions of The Best American Poetry , and featured in publications such as The American Poetry Review , The Paris Review , and Poetry . Shapiro's work as an art critic includes reviews for Art News and The New Yorker , and books on Jim Dine, Jasper Johns, and Piet Mondrian. As a literary critic, he published the first book-length study of John Ashbery's poetry in 1979. His awards include the 1962 Gotham Book Mart Avant-Garde Poetry Award and the 1977 American Academy and Institute of Arts and Letters Morton Dauwen Zabel Award. He was also a National Book Award Finalist for A Man Holding an Acoustic Panel (1971).

From 1991 to 2016, Shapiro was professor of art at William Paterson University, where he began teaching art history in 1985. He also taught English at Columbia University from 1972 to 1981, poetry and literature at the Cooper Union School of Architecture, and visual arts at Princeton University.
In 1970 Shapiro married architect Lindsay Stamm Shapiro. The couple have one son, Daniel, who is also a published poet. Shapiro has lived in Riverdale, New York since the 1970s.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: David Shapiro Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Unusual or non-descriptive titles provided by creator have been retained. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing. Information in brackets is also supplied by staff.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The David Shapiro Papers consists of correspondence, writings, chronological files, personal and professional papers, digital files, and audiovisual materials by or relating to David Shapiro. Correspondence documents his collaborations and friendships with other writers, poets, critics, and artists in the United States and abroad. Correspondents include John Ashbery, Ted Berrigan, Harold Bloom, Joseph Ceravolo, John Ciardi, Arthur A. Cohen, Peter Cole, Tory Dent, Allen Ginsberg, Michal Govrin, Barbara Guest, Donald Hall, John Hejduk, Richard Hell, Jasper Johns, Roman Jakobson, Kenneth Koch, Ann Lauterbach, David Lehman, Frank Lima, Joyce Carol Oates, Frank O'Hara, Ron Padgett, Anne Porter, Fairfield Porter, and Meyer Schapiro. Writings consist of poetry, art and literary criticism, exhibition catalog essays, articles, translations, reviews, and other works by Shapiro, including books on John Ashbery, Jim Dine, Jasper Johns, and Piet Mondrian, and translations of Sonia and Robert Delaunay, and Jacques Dupin. Chronological files contain a mix of correspondence, writings, and other materials organized by year. Personal and professional papers include Shapiro's sticker collages and other artwork, photographs, collected correspondence, and teaching files. Also present in the collection are poetry, prose, and essays by other writers such as Joe Brainard.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Printed material received with the collection was removed for separate cataloging and can be accessed by searching the library's online catalog.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The David Shapiro Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence consists of letters and printed emails to David Shapiro from writers, poets, artists, architects, critics, museum and gallery curators, editors, publishers, students, family, and friends. General correspondence documents Shapiro's relationships with colleagues and friends including critics Harold Bloom and Arthur A. Cohen; poets Joseph Ceravolo, John Ciardi, Peter Cole, Tory Dent, Allen Ginsberg, Michal Govrin, Donald Hall, Richard Hell, Kenneth Koch, Ann Lauterbach, David Lehman, Frank Lima, Frank O'Hara, Ron Padgett, and Anne Porter; architect John Hejduk; and artists Jasper Johns and Fairfield Porter. A small amount of correspondence is enclosed with writings shared by correspondents. Also included are occasional letters and printed email replies from Shapiro. Professional correspondence contains letters pertaining to Shapiro's career as a poet, writer, critic, and teacher, organized by publication, publisher, organization, or subject. Topics include anthologies, publishing contracts, and requests for submissions.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: General Correspondence, and Professional Correspondence. Each is arranged alphabetically by correspondent, with unsorted correspondence filed at the end of the sequence.

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Correspondence can also be found in Series II. Writings, Series III. Chronological Files, and Series IV. Personal and Professional Papers.

did.unittitle: New Jersey State Council on the Arts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writings consist of books, poetry, art and architecture subject files, shorter works, and notebooks and journals documenting David Shapiro's career as a poet, author, critic, editor, and translator. The series also contains writings by others.

Files for books contain notes, research materials, drafts, proofs, publicity and press clippings, reviews, and correspondence related to poetry and prose by Shapiro, as well as works he edited or translated, including his first poetry collection, January: A Book of Poems ; An Anthology of New York Poets , co-edited with Ron Padgett; John Ashbery: An Introduction to the Poetry ; and Jasper Johns Drawings 1954–1984 .

Poetry files contain notes, drafts, broadsides, and copies of Shapiro's published and unpublished poems from approximately 1957 to 2015. Included are collaborations with his wife Lindsay and son Daniel, poems grouped by subject, and selections for poetry readings. A small number of files includes reference materials and copies of Shapiro's prose writings.

Art and architecture subject files consist of Shapiro's reviews, interviews, introductions to exhibitions, and monographs on the work of individual artists and architects such as John Hejduk, Jasper Johns, Piet Mondrian, and Fairfield Porter. Materials include notes, drafts, printed versions, and copies of Shapiro's work as an art critic and historian, as well as reference materials, clippings, exhibition catalogs and ephemera, and correspondence. Also included are files on poet John Ashbery, composer John Cage, and art historian Meyer Schapiro.

Shorter works contain notes, drafts, offprints, published versions, and copies of Shapiro's articles, essays, book and exhibition reviews, short fiction, and plays, as well as publications edited by Shapiro such as New Observations . Outlines, proposals, research materials, interviews, tributes, open letters, and lists are also present. Included are the play Harrisburg Mon Amour , co-written with Stephen Paul Miller with music by Laurie Anderson, interviews with Andy Warhol and John Cage, and essays on Denise Levertov and Elizabeth Bishop.
Notebooks and journals feature Shapiro's writing notes, early drafts, and diary entries about his personal and professional life spanning approximately 1961 to 2007.

Writings of others consist of interviews of Shapiro and translations of his works, as well as drafts, proofs, and printed versions of other writers' poems, prose, plays, articles, and essays. Included are works by Joe Brainard, Ted Berrigan, Tory Dent, Michal Govrin, Donald Hall, Richard Hell, Kenneth Koch, David Lehman, Anne Porter, and Ron Padgett. Also present are copies of self-published or small-press journals and anthologies dating from 1961 to 1994.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six subseries: Books, Poetry, Art and Architecture Subject Files, Shorter Works, Notebooks and Journals, and Writings of Others.

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Writings can also be found in Series III. Chronological Files.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poetry books consist of Shapiro's twelve volumes of poetry published from 1965 to 2017, beginning with his debut, January: A Book of Poems . Other works include Lateness , After a Lost Original , and New and Selected Poems, 1965–2006 .

Prose and edited works contain fourteen books written, edited, or translated by Shapiro from 1970 to 2001, beginning with An Anthology of New York Poets , a collection of New York School poets such as John Ashbery, Ted Berrigan, Kenneth Koch, and Frank O'Hara. Materials for the monograph John Ashbery: An Introduction to the Poetry also include the book's foundational text, Shapiro's doctoral dissertation, "The Meaning of Meaninglessness: The Poetry of John Ashbery." Also included are monographs on artists Jim Dine, Jasper Johns, Piet Mondrian, and Richard Upton; English translations of Robert and Sonia Delaunay's writings, Jacques Dupin's poetry, and Rafael Alberti's poems on Pablo Picasso; and anthologies of poetry by Joseph Ceravolo and Frank Lima edited by Shapiro.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Poetry Books, and Prose and Edited Works. Each is arranged alphabetically by title.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in chronological order, with undated materials filed at the end of the sequence.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by author or folder title, with unsorted materials filed at the end of the sequence.

title.part: The New Freedoms: Contemporary Russian and American Poetry

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chronological Files contain personal and professional correspondence, writings, event ephemera, clippings, reference materials, and other papers. Included are letters and printed email exchanges of David Shapiro and other poets, writers, and artists such as Ron Padgett and Joseph Lease, as well as correspondence with family and friends. Writings in this series include notes, drafts, and copies of Shapiro's poetry and prose, as well as works of others.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of personal papers, collected correspondence and other papers, and teaching files of David Shapiro.

Personal papers contain appointment books, artwork, awards, student records and papers, event ephemera, grants, press clippings, photographs, resumes, subject files, sheet music, and financial documents, among other materials.

Collected correspondence and other papers consist of letters, clippings, photographs, and other files organized in binders by Shapiro. Materials document notable events in his personal and professional life from approximately 1960 to 1993, including high school and university education, career highlights, works published, awards, and public appearances.

Teaching files contain coursework, syllabi, class notes, graded and ungraded student works, reading lists, correspondence, job applications, and other materials related to Shapiro's teaching career at Columbia University, The Cooper Union, Princeton University, William Paterson University, and other institutions. Also present are materials related to his work developing and teaching poetry programs for elementary school students.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three subseries: Personal Papers, Collected Correspondence and Other Papers, and Teaching Files.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 42 (student records): Restricted until 2077. For further information consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by folder title, with unsorted materials filed at the end of the sequence.

did.unittitle: Critical and published references to David Shapiro
Kisufim Jerusalem Conference of Jewish Writers
The New Yorker art critic position
Portfolio for William Paterson University Committee for Faculty Salary Adjustment
Sheet music for "Unwritten" by Mohammed Fairouz
University of Cambridge medieval literature course
Folders with handwritten notes on them

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Shapiro assembled six binders featuring significant correspondence from writers, artists, friends, and family, as well as selected press clippings, event ephemera, drawings, photographs, and other documents relating to his personal and professional life. Notable correspondents include John Ashbery, Ted Berrigan, Harold Bloom, John Ciardi, Arthur A. Cohen, Allen Ginsberg, Barbara Guest, Donald Hall, Roman Jakobson, Jasper Johns, Kenneth Koch, Joyce Carol Oates, Frank O'Hara, Ron Padgett, Anne Porter, Fairfield Porter, and Meyer Schapiro. Also included is a seventh binder pertaining to his wife, architect Lindsay Stamm Shapiro.
Materials were removed from the binders and rehoused in folders during processing.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by folder title, with unsorted materials filed at the end of the sequence.

did.unittitle: Institute for the Advancement of Philosophy for Children materials
Pluralism in Art and Literature proposal
A Poetics of Architecture: Three Lectures

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 42 (student records): Restricted until 2077. For further information consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains computer and audiovisual media including compact discs, DVDs, audiocassettes, and videocassettes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Computer Media, and Audiovisual Media.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 50 (computer media): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies of electronic files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 51 (audiovisual materials): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains audio and video recordings of David Shapiro's poetry readings and interviews conducted with artists.

did.unittitle: "Rosenquist (a part of this slide) and D. Shapiro"

The Writer's Voice 10th Anniversary Season presents "A Poet's Bible" featuring David Bradley, E. M. Broner, Ann Lauterbach, Phillip Lopate, High Nissenson, Grace Schulman, David Shapiro, David Rosenberg
